coredata.dc:title: A ZIF-67-derived lamellar CoP@C cocatalyst for promoting photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junwen
Song, Tao
Su, Lei
Xu, Haoyang
Bai, Xiaoyu
Zhou, Lina
Tu, Weixia
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water is one of the top issues to achieve green hydrogen energy and utilize solar energy. Construction of cocatalyst is a major part for efficient photocatalysts. Lamellar flower-like CoP@C cocatalyst is synthesized via the phosphating of cobalt precursor derived from metal-organic framework ZIF-67. Different from usual phosphating of ZIF-67 directly, a typical solvothermal treatment of ZIF-67 contributes to tuning the formation of C nanodots on the lamellar CoP. CoP@C as cocatalyst exhibits a remarkable role of improving photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. CoP@C/CdS composite shows a photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 164.4 mmol g−1 h−1, which is much higher than those of pure CdS and other CoP/CdS photocatalysts. The heterojunction and interaction are verified between CoP@C and CdS. Light absorption and photoelectric properties of CoP@C/CdS are enhanced accompanying with strong reduction ability. A type-Ⅱ transfer path of photoelectrons is underway in CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst, accelerating the separation of electron-hole pairs and the transfer of carriers, and further resulting in the promoted photocatalytic performance. This work provides a suitable way to achieve carbon nanodots involved metal compound cocatalysts for efficient hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution
Cobalt phosphide
Carbon nanodots
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of lamellar CoP@C nanoflowers from ZIF-67

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of Co(OH)2, CoP and CoP/CdS composites.
SEM images of (a) Co(OH)2, (b) CoP and (c) CoP@C/CdS; (d) Raman spectra of CoP@C/CdS.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM image of CoP@C/CdS; (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of CoP@C; (e-h) EDS elemental mapping images of CoP@C.
XPS spectra of CoP@C/CdS composite: (a) survey, (b) S 2p, (c) Cd 3d (d) P 2p, (e) Co 2p and (f) C 1s electrons.
Photocatalytic rates of H2 evolution over CoP/CdS composites with (a) different CoP, and (b) different loading amount of CoP in CoP@C/CdS composites.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c, d) Mott-Schottky plots of CdS, CoP@C and CoP@C/CdS photocatalysts.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, (b) EIS plots and (c) PL spectra of CdS and CoP@C/CdS composites.
The proposed mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution over the CoP@C/CdS composite.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities for H2 evolution.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: lactic acid as sacrificial agent, 300 W Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm).

head.ce:title: A ZIF-67-derived lamellar CoP@C cocatalyst for promoting photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water is one of the top issues to achieve green hydrogen energy and utilize solar energy. Construction of cocatalyst is a major part for efficient photocatalysts. Lamellar flower-like CoP@C cocatalyst is synthesized via the phosphating of cobalt precursor derived from metal-organic framework ZIF-67. Different from usual phosphating of ZIF-67 directly, a typical solvothermal treatment of ZIF-67 contributes to tuning the formation of C nanodots on the lamellar CoP. CoP@C as cocatalyst exhibits a remarkable role of improving photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. CoP@C/CdS composite shows a photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 164.4 mmol g−1 h−1, which is much higher than those of pure CdS and other CoP/CdS photocatalysts. The heterojunction and interaction are verified between CoP@C and CdS. Light absorption and photoelectric properties of CoP@C/CdS are enhanced accompanying with strong reduction ability. A type-Ⅱ transfer path of photoelectrons is underway in CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst, accelerating the separation of electron-hole pairs and the transfer of carriers, and further resulting in the promoted photocatalytic performance. This work provides a suitable way to achieve carbon nanodots involved metal compound cocatalysts for efficient hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of clean energies, hydrogen energy is considered to be a substitute for fossil fuels. It has wide applications in metallurgical [1], pharmaceutical industries [2], aviation fuel [3] and fuel cells [4] etc. Photocatalytic water reaction is regarded as one of promising ways to gain hydrogen energy. Various semiconductors have been concerned as photocatalysts for hydrogen production from water [5–9]. Due to its proper band gap and conduction band position, cadmium sulfide (CdS) has been studied extensively as photocatalyst. However, rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and photo-corrosion of single component CdS photocatalyst limit its application. Cocatalysts are usually combined to improve photocatalytic activity and stability of CdS [10–16].

Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) are composed of earth-abundant transition metal elements and phosphorus. TMPs have received extensive attention due to their unique physical and chemical properties [17]. They have been widely used in catalytic hydrogenation [18], supercapacitors [19] and batteries [20]. Liu and Rodriguez firstly reported that Ni2P is an efficient cocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [21]. TMPs materials can serve to replace noble metals cocatalysts. Ni2P/g-C3N4 composite with a direct Z-scheme heterojunction showed a 22 times higher photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution than pure g-C3N4 [22]. CoP/CdS hybrid was reported with an improved H2 evolution rate of 13,785 μmol h−1 g−1, which was 9 times as high as pure CdS [23]. Co2P was proven as an efficient cocatalyst in Co2P/CdS composite with a H2 evolution rate of 19,373 μmol h−1 g−1 [24]. Obviously, various TMPs can participate as suitable promoters for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Moreover, it is well known that morphology can remarkably affect activities of photocatalysts. Different from zero-dimensional nanoparticles or one-dimensional nanorods, two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets possess large specific surface area and rich active sites. Moreover, 2D structure of nanosheet materials favors the rapid transfer of photogenerated carriers to surface active sites [25,26]. Therefore, the further exploration of 2D TMPs photocatalysts is suitable for better photocatalytic H2 evolution from water.

Herein, a unique CoP@C structure composed of CoP nanosheets and C nanodots was optimized as cocatalyst for CdS photocatalyst. The precursor construction of Co(OH)2 nanoflowers is through an additional solvothermal treatment of metal-organic framework ZIF-67. The synthesized CoP@C/CdS composites were investigated as photocatalysts in hydrogen production reaction from water. Photocatalytic activities were compared with other cases of different CoP structures (nanoparticles and nanosheets). Characterizations including morphology, structure, photo-responsive property and photocurrent responsibility were investigated. Effect of the additional solvothermal process of ZIF-67 for Co(OH)2 was discussed on the CoP@C structure and photocatalytic performance of CoP@C/CdS. Reaction mechanism of the ternary CoP@C/CdS composite was analysed. This work provides a suitable way to achieve C-doped TMPs cocatalysts for remarkable photocatalytic performance of noble metal-free photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lamellar CoP@C nanoflowers from ZIF-67
ce:section.ce:para: Lamellar CoP@C nanoflowers were synthesized from Co(OH)2 nanoflowers as template. A solvothermal treatment of ZIF-67 was conducted to form Co(OH)2 nanoflowers. ZIF-67 was obtained according to the usual method [27]. Typically, cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 3 mmol) and 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2, 7 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL methanol by ultrasonicating and stirring for 10 min, in which a ZIF-67 suspension formed. Then, the suspension was put into a 100-mL Teflon autoclave to go through a solvothermal process under 120 °C for 12 h. The formed solid product (Co(OH)2 nanoflowers) was washed with deionized water (DI) and ethanol for several times. Then, 500 mg of NaH2PO2 and 50 mg of the synthesized Co(OH)2 were placed in upstream and downstream of a porcelain boat, respectively. The porcelain boat was heated in tube furnace at 300 °C for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained product was washed and dried. The final black powder was named as CoP@C.

Other CoP nanomaterials were considered as cocatalysts for comparison. CoP nanoparticles were synthesized through a direct calcination process of ZIF-67 (after the aging for 12 h at room temperature) with NaH2PO2, without the above solvothermal treatment of ZIF-67. The as-obtained product was named as CoP NPs [28]. In addition, CoP nanosheets (CoP NSs) were compared also from Co(OH)2 nanosheets synthesized without using ZIF-67 [29]. The Co(OH)2 nanosheets were prepared through a hydrothermal procedure at 110 °C for 24 h and the reaction mixture contained cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mmol) and urea (CH4N2O, 20 mmol) in 60 mL DI water.

CdS nanoparticles were synthesized through a hydrothermal method. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 60 mL DI water at 50 °C and then cooled to room temperature. Cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 2 mmol) and thiourea (NH2CSNH2, 4 mmol) were put into the CTAB solution following with 10 mmol KOH added. After the 8 h stirring, the obtained suspension was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon autoclave keeping at 200 °C for 24 h. The formed solid product was washed with DI and ethanol, filtrated and dried at 60 °C. The final yellow powder was CdS nanoparticles.

CoP@C/CdS composites were synthesized by a simple physical mixing method. CdS and CoP@C were dispersed in 60 mL H2O, followed by ultrasonication for 1 h and stirring for 12 h. The obtained powder was washed with DI water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. Several CoP@C/CdS composites were synthesized by varying the molar amount of CoP@C and CdS, which were named as x% CoP@C/CdS (x = 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, x was the molar percentage of CoP@C). Other CoP/CdS composites with CoP NPs and CoP NSs were also prepared for comparison using the same method.

Morphologies of samples were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, HITACHI SU-8020) and field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30). Crystal structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-6000) with a speed of 5°/min over 2θ range from 10° to 90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on Thermo VG ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used to analyze chemical states. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was performed on a Tu-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were gained from measurements on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd.) with the emission wavelength of 370 nm. Content of carbon element was analysed from elementar vairo EL CUBE (elementar analysensysteme GmbH).

Photocatalytic hydrogen production reactions were performed in a quartz container irradiated by a 300 W Xenon lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter. The powder photocatalyst (3 mg) was well dispersed in lactic acid aqueous solution (20 mL, 10 vol%) under 10 min ultrasonication. Then, the reaction solution was bubbled with N2 for 15 min to ensure anaerobic conditions before switching on the lamp. The reaction system was conducted at room temperature. 1 mL of gas from the top of reaction system was sampled every hour and it was analysed by a gas chromatograph (TCD with TDX-1 molecular sieve column, N2 carrier) to determine amount of the generated H2.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were operated on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) with a standard three electrode system using Na2SO4 aqueous solution (0.5 M) as the electrolyte. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The work electrode was prepared as follows. 2 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixture of ethanol (1 mL) and Nafion (5 wt%, 50 μL), sonicating for 30 min to form a uniform suspension. 80 μL of the above suspension was dropped on a 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass, and the obtained electrode was dried in air. The transient photocurrent responses were measured under 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation (>420 nm). Mott-Schottky plots with a frequency of 1 kHz and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were obtained under the same conditions without the light irradiation.

Metal-organic framework ZIF-67 was treated in methanol with a solvothermal process and the obtained solid product was verified to be Co(OH)2. Then, CoP formed through the calcination of Co(OH)2 with NaH2PO2. The physical mixing of the as-synthesized CoP and CdS nanoparticle was performed to obtain CoP/CdS composites. Their crystal structures were investigated through XRD characterizations and the XRD patterns are given in Fig. 1 . Diffraction peaks of Co(OH)2 located at 2θ values of 11.54°, 34.47°, 38.95° indicate (003), (102) and (105) crystal planes of Co(OH)2 (JCPDS No. 46-0605), respectively. Diffraction peaks of CoP centered at 31.54°, 36.24°, 46.19°, 48.12°, 52.27° and 56.83° correspond to (011), (111), (112), (211), (103) and (301) crystal planes of CoP (JCPDS No. 29-0497), respectively. For CoP/CdS composites, the visible peaks at 24.90°, 26.56°, 28.22°, 43.70°, 47.84° and 51.87° belong to (100), (002), (101), (110), (103), (112) crystal planes of the hexagonal CdS (JCPDS No. 41-1049), respectively. Only the characteristic peaks of CdS can be observed in CoP/CdS composites without obvious signals from CoP, which is due to the poor crystallinity and low content of CoP.

Characterizations of field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy were conducted to know about morphologies of the samples. Fig. 2 a shows SEM image of Co(OH)2 derived from ZIF-67 with the additional solvothermal treatment, which indicates a flower-like structure of Co(OH)2 composed of thin nanosheets. The formed CoP nanoflowers with smaller sizes in Fig. 2b duplicate the morphology of Co(OH)2, confirming the phosphating of Co(OH)2 template under the calcination process. Fig. 2c gives SEM image of 10% CoP/CdS composite, which will be verified to be CoP@/CdS composite later. Nanoparticles around 20–30 nm and nanosheets could be observed, which are CdS nanoparticles and CoP nanosheets, respectively. In the composite, CoP exists as nanosheets instead of nanoflowers, which is attributed to the 1-h ultrasonic treatment during the physical mixing of CoP and CdS. The flower-like structure of CoP is peeled off and then it tightly combines with CdS nanoparticles. CoP@C/CdS composite in the subsequent content is 10% CoP@C/CdS unless otherwise specified. TEM image of CoP@C/CdS is given in Fig. 3 a, indicating the tightly anchored CdS nanoparticles on the surface of CoP nanosheets. According to its HRTEM image (Fig. 3b), the lattice fringe of 0.359 nm can be assigned to (100) crystal plane of CdS, and the lattice fringes of 0.207 nm and 0.279 nm belong to (210) and (002) crystal planes of CoP, respectively. SEM and TEM results confirm the tight contacts between CoP and CdS. The formed heterostructures in CoP@C/CdS composite would promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers to improve its photocatalytic activity.

Particularly, seen from Fig. 3a, there is a trace number of small-sized nanoparticles on CoP nanosheets as circled in red. For clarity, CoP was observed by HRTEM as shown in Fig. 3c and d. The small nanoparticles (6–8 nm) on CoP are verified to be carbon nanodots with the observed lattice fringe of 0.23 nm from (002) crystal plane. In addition, EDS elemental mapping images of CoP@C as shown in Fig. 3e and f clearly indicate the co-existence of C, Co and P elements. The carbon nanodots may be derived from the remaining carbon from ZIF-67. Element analysis of CoP gives 0.78 atom% of carbon involved. In fact, Raman spectra of CoP given in Fig. 2d indicate the co-existence of CoP and C. The weak D and G peaks of C nanodots are visible. Therefore, the ternary CoP@C/CdS composite is constructed.

XPS spectra of CoP@C/CdS composite are shown in Fig. 4 to understand surface compositions and chemical states. The survey spectra in Fig. 4a indicate the existence of Co, Cd, S, P, C and O elements in CoP@C/CdS. The high-resolution spectra of S 2p in Fig. 4b can be divided into two peaks with binding energies of 161.9 eV and 163.1 eV for S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, corresponding to S2− in CdS [30]. Peaks centered at binding energies of 405.1 eV and 411.9 eV in Fig. 4c can be assigned to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 electrons of Cd2+ in CdS, respectively [31]. Peak fittings of P 2p spectra (Fig. 4d) indicate peaks at 129.0 eV and 130.0 eV corresponding to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of CoP, respectively, and peaks at 113.3 eV and 134.5 eV attributing to P–C and P–O [32]. XPS spectra of Co 2p are displayed in Fig. 4e. The peaks at 778.8 eV and 793.5 eV are from Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 electrons, accompanying with satellite peaks at 785.1 eV and 806.2 eV. The XPS results suggest the coexistence of CoP and CdS. Moreover, the fitting peaks from C 1s exhibit the signals from CO, C–N, C–C and C–Co, which locate at 289.0 eV, 286.9 eV, 284.8 eV as well as 283.4 eV, respectively [33], which identifies the existence of carbon as C nanodots in the CoP@C/CdS composite.

Photocatalytic activities of the synthesized CoP@C/CdS composites were investigated in hydrogen evolution reaction from water under visible light irradiation. Other CoP nanomaterials, including CoP nanoparticles (NPs) from ZIF-67 directly and CoP nanosheets (NSs) from Co(OH)2 nanosheets, were used to form CoP/CdS composites for comparison. Effects of different CoP nanomaterials on photocatalytic activities were discussed. As shown in Fig. 5 a, photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of CoP@C/CdS is higher than those of CoP/CdS (NPs) and CoP/CdS (NSs). Both CoP@C and CoP NPs were synthesized using ZIF-67 as cobalt precursor. The difference is that CoP@C formed from Co(OH)2 nanoflowers obtained after an additional solvothermal process on ZIF-67. Under the solvothermal treatment, the unstable structure of ZIF-67 tended to collapse to form Co(OH)2 nanoflowers and the small amount of carbon remained to form C nanodots in the following phosphating process. The formed Co(OH)2 nanoflowers serve as template for the formation of CoP@C nanoflowers. The CoP@C nanoflowers provide CoP@C nanosheets after ultrasonication, which are much thinner than CoP (NSs) through the phosphating of Co(OH)2 nanosheet from cobalt nitrate and urea [29]. Obviously, the transfer from ZIF-67 to Co(OH)2 through the present way is essential for the presence of both CoP thin nanosheets and C nanodots in CoP@C/CdS composite. The ternary structure of CoP@C/CdS exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. Due to the thermal decomposition of ZIF during the phosphating process, CoP NPs synthesized directly from ZIF-67 would be supposed to contain much more amount of carbon component [28]. Therefore, the typical trace of carbon nanodots in CoP has the role of promoting photocatalytic activity of CoP@C/CdS composite.

Effect of CoP@C amount on photocatalytic activities of CoP@C/CdS composites was further discussed. Pure CdS, CoP@C/CdS and CoP@C were used as photocatalysts for H2 production, and their average H2 evolution rates are displayed in Fig. 5b. No obvious hydrogen could be detected in the reaction over CoP@C because of its narrow band gap. Pure CdS shows a low H2 evolution rate of 3.1 mmol g−1 h−1. The CoP@C/CdS composites exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activities which are much higher than pure CdS. The maximum H2 evolution rate occurs on 10% CoP@C/CdS, with the value of 164.4 mmol g−1 h−1 (53 times as CdS). The H2 evolution rate tends to decrease along with the further increase amount of CoP@C, due to excessive covering of CoP@C on the surface of CdS and the resulted hindrance of light absorption. An appropriate loading of CoP@C improves the photocatalytic performance of CoP@C/CdS greatly.

Optical properties of CdS, CoP@C and CoP@C/CdS composites were characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, CdS has a visible light absorption edge around 540 nm and the light absorption is low above 600 nm. CoP@C exhibits the strong absorption ability in the range of 300–800 nm. CoP@C/CdS composites have the stronger absorption ability than CdS and their absorption edges are red shifted to the higher wavelength. Band gap energy (Eg) can be deduced from UV–vis spectra through Kubelka-Munk method as given in Fig. 6b. CoP@C possesses a narrow Eg of 1.80 eV (the inset of Fig. 6b). Egs of CdS, 5% CoP@C/CdS, 10% CoP@C/CdS and 20% CoP@C/CdS are 2.29 eV, 2.20 eV, 2.18 eV, and 2.10 eV, respectively. The decreased Egs of CoP@C/CdS composites with the increasing amount of CoP@C indicate the enhanced absorption of visible light. Conduction band potentials (ECB) of CdS, CoP@C and CoP@C/CdS composites can be determined from Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots in Fig. 6c and d. The positive slopes indicate n-type semiconductors. The plat band potential is close to ECB for n-type semiconductors [34]. Plat band potentials of CdS and CoP@C are −0.58 V and −0.76 V (vs. NHE), respectively. Therefore, valence band potential (EVB) values of CdS and CoP@C are 1.71 V and 1.04 V (vs. NHE) according to the equation of EVB = ECB + Eg. The more negative ECB of CoP@C/CdS than CdS would provide photoelectrons with the stronger reduction ability for hydrogen evolution [35].

Electrochemical measurements are involved to analyze the transfer of photogenerated carriers in photocatalysts. Transient photocurrent responses of photocatalysts under 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation (>420 nm) with several on-off cycles are given in Fig. 7 a. The high photocurrent means much amount of photogenerated carriers with the high transfer efficiency. All CoP@C/CdS composites possess the higher photocurrents than CdS, and 10% CoP@C/CdS reaches its maximum. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of photocatalysts are given in Fig. 7b. The arc radius of 10% CoP@C/CdS composite is the smallest, which implies the lowest Faraday impedance and the rapidest transfer of photogenerated carriers superior to CdS and other CoP@C/CdS composites. Fig. 7c shows photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CdS and CoP@C/CdS photocatalysts under the excitation light of 370 nm and the emission peaks located at 522 nm are visible. Intensity of the emission peak from 10% CoP@C/CdS is much lower than those from others, indicating the faster separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole carriers. The 10% CoP@C/CdS composite is verified to display the high transient photocurrent response, the low impedance and the low recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which contributes to its remarkable photocatalytic performance for H2 evolution.

According to their lattice fringes in the HRTEM image and electrochemical measurements, n-n heterojunctions formed between CdS and CoP@C, which is beneficial to accelerate the transfer of photogenerated carriers. Based on the energy band alignments of CdS and CoP@C, a mechanism of hydrogen evolution from water is proposed as shown in Fig. 8 . Both CdS and CoP@C could be excited by visible light to generate electrons and holes. Due to the more negative conduction band (CB) value of CoP, photogenerated electrons in CB of CoP tend to transfer to CB of CdS, while holes in valence band (VB) of CdS tend to transfer to VB of CoP. Because of the presence of C nanodots on CoP nanosheets, another transfer way is provided for the transfer of photoinduced electrons from CoP to C nanodots. The accelerated migration of electrons between heterojunctions would promote the separation and the transfer of photoinduced carriers and significantly extend their lifetimes. The introduce of CoP@C also favors to enhancing the intensity of light absorption. Reduction reaction for hydrogen occurs on conduction bands of CdS and the surface of C nanodots. Lactic acid would be oxidized with the holes on valence bands of CdS and CoP. Thereby, the ternary CoP@C/CdS composite exhibits much high photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution.

Besides, the recycling reactions were carried out to study the stability of the CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst and the activities are given in Fig. 9 . The H2 evolution rate decreases after the first cycle and keeps constant in the next three recycles, indicating the good stability of CoP@C/CdS. Comparing with similar photocatalysts in other literatures as listed in Table 1 , the present CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst exhibits a remarkable and high photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. The participate of CoP@C significantly promotes the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and transfer of carriers, resulting in the enhanced H2 evolution performance of CoP@C/CdS. When CdS nanorods were used instead of CdS nanoparticles in the physical mixing with CoP@C, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of the formed CoP@C/CdS(nanorods) is much high as 264.6 mmol g−1 h−1. Definitely, the solvothermal treatment of metal organic framework provides a simple and effective way to gain superior cocatalyst containing C nanodots and TMPs for improving H2 evolution activities of photocatalysts.

A typical CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst was constructed for efficient hydrogen evolution from water. The additional solvothermal treatment of ZIF-67 was provided to achieve cobalt precursor Co(OH)2, which results in the formation of CoP with suitable C nanodots (6–8 nm) in the subsequent phosphating. Lamellar flower-like CoP@C served as cocatalyst and CoP@C/CdS photocatalyst exhibited much higher photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution than CdS and other CoP/CdS photocatalysts. The 10% CoP/CdS had the highest hydrogen evolution rate of 164.4 mmol g−1 h−1, which is 53 times higher than that of CdS. The coexistence of CoP and carbon nanodots and the heterostructures between CdS and CoP@C could significantly promote the transfer of photogenerated electrons and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The enhanced photo-response properties and strong reduction ability of CoP@C/CdS were verified for its superior activity of hydrogen evolution. The present solvothermal treatment of ZIF is a feasible and simple way to construct carbon nanodots on photocatalysts to improve their photocatalytic activities.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078016) is acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review on the hydro metallurgical process for the production of nickel and copper powders by hydrogen reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Agrawal, V. Kumar, B. D. Pandey, K. K. Sahu, A comprehensive review on the hydro metallurgical process for the production of nickel and copper powders by hydrogen reduction. Mater. Res. Bull. 41(4) (2006) 879-892.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress toward hydrogen medicine: potential of molecular hydrogen for preventive and therapeutic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ohta, Recent progress toward hydrogen medicine: potential of molecular hydrogen for preventive and therapeutic applications. Curr. Pharm. Design 17(22) (2011) 2241-2252.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Life cycle evaluation of hydrogen and other potential fuels for aircrafts
